10.1016/j.jmp.2005.06.005
Numerical representation of preferences by real-valued functionals is a fundamental tool in modelling many choice problems arising in Decision Theory, Economics, Statistics and Psychology (both theoretical and applied). Initially, the effort of representing such preferences was restricted to complete preorders (e.g. von Neumann–Morgerstern and Savage expected utility theories). On the other hand, there are several reasons why one would study incomplete preferences. First, there are many instances (e.g. stochastic dominance, coalitional bargaining games) in which decisions derive from the—possibly different—preferences of several individuals, so that the “collective” preference is not complete. Second, there are many problems in which, from a subjective point of view, it is natural to assume that a decision maker (DM) cannot always compare two acts since, owing to his partial knowledge, he is not able to supply any event with a probability assessment. Thus, the study of incomplete preferences is not taken up for sake of mathematical generalization but to supply a rational model of choice in many real problems.

In order to frame incomplete preferences in a setting of expected utility, one can find in the literature two different “dual” approaches. The first one, following a suggestion of von Neumann and Morgerstern, describes the incompleteness by multiple “linear” utilities, so that it can be seen as a  multiperson-utility perspective ; see, for instance,  Aumann (1962) ,  Kannai (1963) ,  Fishburn (1975) ,  Shapley and Baucells (1998) ,  Ok (2002)  and  Dubra, Maccheroni, and Ok (2004) . The second one instead, following some Knight's ideas, describes the incompleteness of preferences by multiple priors, so that it can be seen as a  subjective ambiguity perspective ; see, for instance,  Giron and Rios (1980) ,  Bewley (2002, original version 1986) ,  Girotto and Holzer (2003) ,  Ghirardato, Maccheroni, Marinacci, and Siniscalchi (2003)  and  Ghirardato, Maccheroni, and Marinacci (2004) .

We also recall two papers, regarding incomplete preferences, which are not directly related with the previous perspectives.  Rumbos (2001)  explains the incompleteness of suitable linear subjective preferences on (a space of) random variables in terms of pointwise comparison of conditional expectations given a fixed finite field of events.  Seidenfeld, Schervish, and Kadane (1995) , in order to get a decision theoretic foundation for robust Bayesian statistics, supply an interesting explanation of incompleteness (in a finite state space setting) in terms of multiple probability/utility pairs; so that, in a sense, this paper can be fitted into a multiperson perspective (without ambiguity).

Now, we supply some comments to clarify the ideas underlying the formal treatment of this paper, in which we adopt a subjective ambiguity perspective. But, in this way, one implicitly assumes a pessimistic attitude of the DM towards resolution of uncertainty, since he will choose decision rules which guard against “catastrophic” consequences; in a sense, one would say that he perceives Nature as an “enemy”. On the other hand, if one thinks that Nature is “neutral”, a reasonable criterion of choice is to adopt an “unanimity principle”, i.e. to prefer

Note that this unanimity principle assures that any optimal choice can be seen as a standard Bayesian rule w.r.t. every prior compatible with the partial knowledge of the DM, as well.

Starting from this example, we introduce a decision model with partial information in an Anscombe–Aumann framework. Following some suggestions of  Bewley (2002) , we consider an arbitrary state space  
                         Ω 
                       and an arbitrary set  X  of prizes (or rewards). Then, we call (roulette) lottery any finitely additive probability on a given field of subsets of  X  and call act (or horse lottery) any mapping that assigns a lottery to each state of Nature. Now, assume that the DM can always compare two arbitrary prizes and that not all prizes are equivalent. Then, introduce the set of bounded lotteries (i.e. lotteries that distribute the whole mass on closed intervals of prizes) and suppose that the DM is concerned in comparing equibounded acts (i.e. acts for which, in any state of Nature, all possible prizes are never better or worse than two given ones (depending on the act but not on the state)). Moreover, keeping in mind the distinction between risk and uncertainty due to  Knight (1921) , assume that the DM represents his risk attitude by a (cardinal) utility function  u  on  X  and is able, owing to his ignorance, to give a probabilistic valuation  
                         μ 
                       
                      only  on an additive family of events in  
                         Ω 
                      . Finally, suppose that the DM describes all admissible scenarios, suggested by his uncertainty and consistent with his partial knowledge, by a suitable set  
                         Π

                         Ω

                      . Of course, if the set  
                         Π 
                       is a singleton, there is no uncertainty; hence, in a sense, the size of this set may be seen as a measure of the uncertainty of the DM about events in  
                         Ω 
                      .

In order to justify the (minimal) additive-class requirement for the family of events on which the DM is able to give a probabilistic assessment, note that it is very natural to assume that if the DM can evaluate two disjoint events, then he can value their union and their complements, as well. Moreover, one of the reasons for confining ourselves to bounded lotteries is that this choice seems to be the most natural extension of the “standard” case (in which  X  contains both a most preferred element and a least preferred one), in order to guarantee the existence of a cardinal utility function which is not forced to be bounded, as required in many economic and/or statistical models (e.g. recall log utility functions). Furthermore, the finitely additive setting adopted in this paper is suggested by the inadequacy of a countable additive point of view for many models in Economics and Psychology.

The aim of the paper is to characterize these preferences on equibounded acts by means of some simple and natural qualitative properties, in order to supply a rational foundation for the behaviour of the DM who adopts the unanimity principle in front of ambiguity. In this way, we also provide a qualitative framework for sensitivity analysis in statistical decision theory, describing under what conditions one may undertake the natural approach of perturbing the priors compatibly with partial knowledge. Contrary to all mentioned papers, we do not assume any hypothesis about the cardinality and the topological structure concerning the space of states of Nature and/or the set of prizes and lotteries, so that this approach can be seen, in a sense, as an “algebraic” (rather than “topological”) axiomatic treatment of preferences.

Stemming from Knight's intuitions about investor behaviour, Bewley (version 1986), to the best of our knowledge, first supplies a rigorous foundation of incomplete preferences in terms of risk and uncertainty; to this end, he assumes the state space and the set of prizes both finite and considers a “partial” prior on a field of events. Consequently, our paper can be seen as a natural generalization of this important result.

Now, we briefly describe two papers that share features, objectives, or methods with this paper. In a rough sense, the major conceptual difference between our approach and the ones underlying these papers is that we consider, according to a Bayesian intuition, a probabilistic assessment of the DM only on some events in the state space and describe then his preferences by families of priors compatible with this “partial” prior. Furthermore, from a structural point of view, all these papers assume the state space finite or preferences defined on simple acts.

Ghirardato et al. (2003)  consider a finite state space and a connected compact topological space  Y  of consequences and a preference on the set of all simple acts with values in  Y . In order to state the independence axiom, they introduce a subjective mixture operation for consequences (via a suitable preference assumption assuring the existence of a utility index on  Y ). Then, by standard axioms, the authors characterize, in the same spirit of the unanimity principle, preferences on simple acts in terms of a (unique) convex and closed set of probabilities (see Proposition 4). We note that, in our setting,  Y  is the set of all bounded lotteries (which may not be seen, in general, as a connected and compact space) and the utility index is the expected utility valued by a cardinal utility function  u ; moreover, unlike these authors, to state the independence property we will adopt the usual objective mixture operation for lotteries.

Ghirardato et al. (2004)  consider an arbitrary state space and a convex set  Y  of consequences (e.g. the set of all bounded lotteries) and a complete preference (satisfying some standard axioms and a weaker form of independence) on the set of all simple measurable acts with values in  Y . Starting from the well-known Ellsberg's experiment, their objective is to formalize the idea that the presence of ambiguity (and the ambiguity attitude of the DM) is revealed to an external observer in the form of violations of the independence axiom. Precisely, the authors consider the maximal restriction of the underlying preference satisfying independence (called unambiguous preference relation), which is clearly incomplete in most cases. Then, they show that this new preference admits a representation, in the same spirit of the unanimity principle, in terms of a utility index on  Y  and a (unique) convex and weak*compact set of probabilities (see Proposition 5).

For sake of completeness, we recall that  Giron and Rios (1980)  and  Girotto and Holzer (2003)  consider potentially incomplete preferences on suitable families of random numbers and characterize (always in the spirit of unanimity principle) those preferences which can be derived, via expectation operator, from a (unique) convex pointwise closed set of probabilities; note that these preferences reveal a risk-neutral attitude of the DM. Though any random number can be seen as an act associating each state of Nature with a point mass on the real line, we remark that this characterization cannot be deduced from the results of this paper (indeed random numbers are not necessarily bounded).

The paper is organized as follows. In  Section 2 , we introduce the set-up, notation, and two subjective orderings depending on the preferences of the DM on constant acts and on his partial probabilistic knowledge about the events in  
                            Ω 
                         ; moreover, we show how to update the preferences when some relevant event has occurred. In  Section 2 , we define coherent preferences as ones satisfying some very natural properties (called basic properties) and state the main results of the paper. The first result allows one to represent (according to the unanimity principle) any coherent preference by a cardinal utility  u  (bounded or not) on the set of prizes and by a convex pointwise closed family  
                            Π 
                          of priors, each one compatible with the partial probability assessment  
                            μ 
                         ; moreover, the same pair  
                            ( 
                            u 
                            , 
                            Π 
                            ) 
                          represents, via conditional expectation, the updated preferences, as well. Finally, when the state space is decomposable into a finite family of  
                            μ 
                         -valuable relevant events on which any updated preference is complete, then the uncertainty “disappears”, i.e. there is only one prior in  
                            Π 
                          (therefore, this result can be seen as a natural generalization of the classical one regarding complete preferences). The second result characterizes coherent preferences as orderings which can be represented in terms of cardinal utilities and families (not necessarily convex and pointwise closed) of priors extending  
                            μ 
                         ; moreover, it points out that there is a unique of such families which is convex and pointwise closed. The last result, rephrasing the previous one, presents in some detail the benchmark case in which the probabilistic knowledge of the DM is minimal. An appendix, in which we supply all the proofs, concludes the paper. In Appendix  A.1 , we determine the cardinal utility  u  representing the preferences on constant acts. In Appendix  A.2 , we show how to obtain, by a cone-separation argument, the convex pointwise closed family  
                            Π 
                          of priors. Finally, in Appendix  A.3 , we prove the main results.

                      We briefly supply some comments about the ideas underlying these axioms (mostly present in the literature). The restricted completeness property is suggested by the Knightian vision of uncertainty and points out that the DM can  always  compare two constant acts, i.e. situations in which the DM knows a priori the probability distribution he will use to determine a resulting consequence. The properties of independence and subjective monotonicity assure that the considered preorder is compatible with the mixture set structure of  
                         A 
                       and with the subjective ordering. Note that subjective monotonicity guarantees the validity of the usual monotonicity axiom: 
                         
                            
                               
                                  P 
                                  
                                     
                                        5 
                                     
                                     
                                        ′ 
                                     
                                  
                               
                            
                            (Monotonicity): If  
                                  a 
                                  ⩾ 
                                  b 
                               , then  
                                  a 
                                  ≽ 
                                  b 
                               . 
                         
                      The continuity property asserts the “conservation of preferences” under the passage to the limit, in the sense that if  
                         a 
                         
                            
                               λ 
                            
                            
                               n 
                            
                         
                         b 
                         ≽ 
                         c 
                       and  
                         
                            
                               λ 
                            
                            
                               n 
                            
                         
                         → 
                         λ 
                      , then  
                         a 
                         λ 
                         b 
                         ≽ 
                         c 
                      . The dominance property assures that a lottery is preferred to another one, whenever all its possible prizes are better than the other lottery. Finally, the last property, considered in  Bewley (2002) , points out that any act, built as a compound lottery of two lotteries  
                         δ 
                         , 
                         
                            
                               δ 
                            
                            
                               ′ 
                            
                         
                       by a bet on an event  F  in  
                         Ω 
                       occurring with known probability  
                         μ 
                         ( 
                         F

                      , is equivalent to the constant act corresponding to the lottery

                         ( 
                         F 
                         )

                         ( 
                         1 
                         - 
                         μ

                         F 
                         )

Now, we are going to show that any coherent preference can be represented by a cardinal utility function on  X  (characterizing the preferences of the DM on constant acts) and a suitable family of  priors  (i.e. finitely additive probabilities on  
                         
                            
                               2 
                            
                            
                               Ω 
                            
                         
                      ). To this end, we need a notion of finitely additive integral, which becomes the usual Lebesgue integral in a countable additive setting; precisely, we will adopt a Dunford–Schwartz-type integral, called  D-integral  (see  Bhaskara Rao & Bhaskara Rao (1983, Chapter 4)  for definition and some properties). Therefore, in the sequel, the expectation  
                         
                            
                               E 
                            
                            
                               ν 
                            
                         
                         ( 
                         f 
                         ) 
                       is the D-integral of the real mapping  f  w.r.t. the probability  
                         ν 
                      . Moreover, we recall that a set of priors is called  pointwise closed  when it is closed under the product topology on  
                         
                            
                               R 
                            
                            
                               
                                  
                                     2 
                                  
                                  
                                     Ω 
                                  
                               
                            
                         
                      .  
                         Theorem 3.1 
                         
                            Assume  
                            
                               ≽ 
                             
                            to be a coherent preference. Then ,  there is a strictly increasing real mapping u on X  ( unique up to a positive affine transformation )  and a convex pointwise closed set  
                            
                               Π 
                               ≠ 
                               ∅ 
                             
                            of priors ,  which are extensions of  
                            
                               μ 
                            ,  such that : 
                               
                                  (i) 
                                  
                                     
                                        a 
                                        ≽ 
                                        b 
                                      
                                     iff  
                                     
                                        
                                           
                                              E 
                                           
                                           
                                              π 
                                           
                                        
                                        [ 
                                        
                                           
                                              E 
                                           
                                           
                                              a 
                                              ( 
                                              ω 
                                              ) 
                                           
                                        
                                        (

On noting that any complete preference is conditionally complete on the trivial partition  
                         P 
                         = 
                         { 
                         Ω 
                         } 
                         ⊂ 
                         F 
                      , the last statement of the theorem assures that completeness is equivalent to conditional completeness on a finite partition  
                         P 
                       whose elements are all assessable by the DM (i.e.  
                         P 
                         ⊂ 
                         F 
                      ). Since, in many situations it is easier verifying completeness for conditional preferences, this result may also have a appreciable normative value.

We point out that independence, continuity, restricted completeness and dominance assure the existence of a cardinal utility function  u  on  X  characterizing the preferences of the DM on constant acts (i.e.

                         ⩾

                                     δ

                         u

                      ). In order to compare this result to some considered in the literature (see, for instance,  Karni & Schmeidler, 1991; Fishburn, 1982 ), note that, for complete preferences, the assumption of P1, P2 and P4 is substantially equivalent to require independence, Archimedean and dominance axioms; moreover, the set of bounded lotteries is closed under finite convex combinations and under conditioning (as usually assumed for families of lotteries). Nevertheless, with reference to Section 3.2 in  Fishburn (1982) , the existence of utility function  u  cannot be derived, in our context, neither from Theorem 1 ( 
                         Δ 
                       is not, in general, closed under  countable  convex combinations), nor from Theorem 2 (we do not assume, quoting Fishburn, the “inelegant” axiom A5). We also remark that we have added subjective monotonicity and coherence to the previous properties in order to build the convex pointwise closed set  
                         Π 
                       of priors extending  
                         μ 
                      .

                         Π 
                       any convex pointwise closed family of priors (extending  
                         μ 
                      ) representing the coherent preference  
                         ≽ 
                      .

Note that the family of priors  
                         Π 
                       is, in a sense, the maximal family of priors which represent the uncertainty of the DM about the events in  
                         Ω 
                      ; moreover, the DM can describe the ambiguity, that he faces in the decision problem, univocally by pointwise closed convex families of priors.

(iii) We prove only that  
                                  Π 
                                is pointwise closed. Let  
                                  { 
                                  
                                     
                                        π 
                                     
                                     
                                        d 
                                     
                                  
                                  ; 
                                  d 
                                  ∈ 
                                  D 
                                  } 
                                be a net in  
                                  Π 
                                pointwise converging to  
                                  π 
                               . Plainly,  
                                  π 
                                is a prior. Consider then the continuous linear functional  
                                  ℓ 
                                  = 
                                  
                                     
                                        E 
                                     
                                     
                                        π 
                                     
                                  
                               . We claim that the net  
                                  { 
                                  
                                     
                                        E 
                                     
                                     
                                        
                                           
                                              π 
                                           
                                           
                                              d 
                                           
                                        
                                     
                                  
                                  ; 
                                  d 
                                  ∈ 
                                  D 
                                  } 
                                converges to  
                                  ℓ 
                                under the weak*topology. Indeed, consider the normal space  
                                  Ω 
                                equipped with the discrete topology and note that, in this setting, weak convergence of finitely additive probabilities is equivalent, by Characterization  Theorem 3.3  in  Girotto and Holzer (1993) , to pointwise convergence. Consequently, since any bounded function on  
                                  Ω 
                                is continuous in the discrete topology, the net  
                                  { 
                                  
                                     
                                        E 
                                     
                                     
                                        
                                           
                                              π 
                                           
                                           
                                              d 
                                           
                                        
                                     
                                  
                                  ; 
                                  d 
                                  ∈ 
                                  D 
                                  } 
                                converges to  
                                  ℓ 
                                under the weak*topology. This proves the claim. On noting that the net  
                                  { 
                                  
                                     
                                        E 
                                     
                                     
                                        
                                           
                                              π 
                                           
                                           
                                              d 
                                           
                                        
                                     
                                  
                                  ; 
                                  d 
                                  ∈ 
                                  D

The necessity immediately follows from  Theorem 3.1 . By straightforward calculation, the converse implication easily follows; note that  Remark A.4  assures the D-integrability of  
                                  
                                     
                                        u 
                                     
                                     
                                        ˜

                                  μ 
                               . The uniqueness of  
                                  
                                     
                                        u 
                                     
                                     
                                        ˜ 
                                     
                                  
                               , up to a positive affine transformation, immediately follows from  Theorem A.5 .
